$comment: Baked FOOD–DRUG interaction dataset (issue #154, extends #144). Per-ITEM food guidance — unlike the drug–drug checker (lib/drug-interactions.json) each entry fires off a SINGLE item (a medication or supplement), needing no second drug. HAND-MAINTAINED (a few dozen canonical, high-severity pairs cover the ground — no generator, mirroring the supplement-catalog style); keep it human-reviewable and cited. Facts from PUBLIC-DOMAIN sources: FDA Structured Product Labeling / DailyMed (the label's drug-interactions / patient-counseling sections are a public-domain U.S. Government work), NIH MedlinePlus / NLM, and RxNorm ingredient CUIs (public-domain NLM vocabulary). No copyrighted commercial database (Micromedex/Lexicomp/FDB/Multum) is vendored. Each entry is keyed on RxNorm ingredient CUIs (authoritative) with a normalized name/synonym fallback, mirroring lib/drug-interactions.json. INFORMATIONAL, never prescriptive — 'discuss with your prescriber or pharmacist'; absence of an entry does NOT mean a food is safe with a drug.

interactions.advice: Avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice — they raise statin blood levels and the risk of muscle injury.
interactions.mechanism: Grapefruit inhibits intestinal CYP3A4, slowing statin breakdown so more drug reaches the bloodstream and myopathy/rhabdomyolysis becomes more likely.

interactions.advice: Avoid grapefruit juice — it can sharply raise blood levels and cause a large drop in blood pressure.
interactions.mechanism: Grapefruit inhibits intestinal CYP3A4, increasing exposure to these dihydropyridine calcium-channel blockers and the risk of hypotension.

interactions.advice: Avoid grapefruit — it sharply raises drug levels and the risk of toxicity.
interactions.mechanism: Grapefruit inhibits intestinal CYP3A4, increasing exposure to these narrow-therapeutic-index immunosuppressants.

interactions.advice: Keep vitamin K intake steady — don't suddenly increase leafy greens (kale, spinach, broccoli); big swings change how well warfarin works.
interactions.mechanism: Vitamin K directly opposes warfarin's anticoagulant effect, so inconsistent dietary intake destabilizes the INR.

interactions.advice: Limit alcohol — it can unpredictably raise or lower warfarin's effect and add to bleeding risk.
interactions.mechanism: Alcohol alters warfarin metabolism (acutely inhibiting, chronically inducing) and independently impairs clotting, destabilizing the INR.

interactions.food: Coffee, dairy, calcium, and high-fiber foods
interactions.advice: Take on an empty stomach with water 30–60 min before eating; keep coffee, dairy, calcium, and iron ≥4 hours apart — they cut absorption.
interactions.mechanism: Food, coffee, and polyvalent cations (calcium, iron) bind levothyroxine in the gut and reduce how much is absorbed, lowering its effect.

interactions.advice: Don't take with dairy, calcium, iron, or antacids — they bind the antibiotic and can make it fail. Separate by ≥2 hours.
interactions.mechanism: Calcium, magnesium, iron, and other polyvalent cations chelate tetracyclines in the gut, sharply reducing absorption.

interactions.food: Dairy, calcium, iron, magnesium, and antacids
interactions.advice: Separate from dairy, calcium, iron, magnesium, and antacids by ≥2 hours — they bind the antibiotic and reduce absorption.
interactions.mechanism: Polyvalent cations chelate fluoroquinolones in the gut, markedly reducing absorption and antibacterial effect.

interactions.advice: Avoid all alcohol during treatment and for 3 days after — the combination causes flushing, nausea, vomiting, and a racing heartbeat.
interactions.mechanism: Metronidazole inhibits acetaldehyde dehydrogenase, producing a disulfiram-like reaction when alcohol is consumed.

interactions.food: Tyramine-rich foods (aged cheese, cured meats, draft beer)
interactions.advice: Avoid tyramine-rich foods — aged cheeses, cured or smoked meats, fermented soy, and draft beer — they can trigger a dangerous blood-pressure spike.
interactions.mechanism: MAO inhibitors block the breakdown of dietary tyramine, which can release stored norepinephrine and cause a hypertensive crisis.

interactions.advice: Go easy on potassium-based salt substitutes and large amounts of high-potassium foods — with an ACE inhibitor/ARB they can push potassium too high.
interactions.mechanism: ACE inhibitors and ARBs reduce potassium excretion, so a big potassium load (especially salt substitutes) can cause hyperkalemia.

interactions.advice: Avoid potassium-based salt substitutes and go easy on high-potassium foods — these diuretics already raise potassium and the combination can become dangerous.
interactions.mechanism: Potassium-sparing diuretics retain potassium, so an added dietary potassium load can cause hyperkalemia and arrhythmia.

interactions.advice: Avoid regular or heavy alcohol — combined with acetaminophen it raises the risk of liver damage. Stay within the daily acetaminophen limit.
interactions.mechanism: Chronic alcohol induces CYP2E1, increasing the toxic acetaminophen metabolite (NAPQI) and the risk of liver injury.

interactions.food: Any food or drink except plain water; calcium and coffee
interactions.advice: Take first thing in the morning with plain water only, stay upright, and wait ≥30 minutes before any food, coffee, or calcium — food blocks nearly all absorption.
interactions.mechanism: Oral bisphosphonates are very poorly absorbed; any food, coffee, or calcium in the gut reduces the already-low absorption to almost nothing.
